video_title: Sports City: The World Cup Keeps Getting Bigger
transcript: Costs a little over $9 for a round trip to MetLife Stadium on the new Jersey transit today at the World Cup. In a couple of weeks, they're going to tax you $98. At this point, I just think I'm going to take the hundred $50 Uber. I'm Randall Williams, a sports business reporter at Bloomberg News, and I'm taking you inside the world's biggest sporting events. Today we are headed to the most watched showdown on earth, the FIFA World Cup. The 22nd FIFA World Cup will be hosted in three countries the United States, Mexico and Canada across 16 cities with 48 teams competing. The most ever. And I haven't even mentioned the stars Messi, Ronaldo and Lamine Yamal, Erling Haaland, Harry Kane and Captain America Christian Pulisic all leading their countries on the pitch as they battle for the world Championship. Fans from across the globe will pour into North America, rooting for their home countries, packing out stadiums, spending big money in host cities. And it all starts on Thursday, June 11th, in Mexico City. And this event is set to be bigger, grander and more lucrative than any Super Bowl, World Series or NBA finals. The World Cup traditionally featured 32 teams in 64 total matches. This year, for the first time ever, there will be 48 teams in the field for a grand total of 104 matches. Fox and Telemundo are paying fee for more than $1 billion to air the tournament. With billions more tuning in and the storylines for countries this year are endless. Can Messi and Argentina defend their title? And can't France, Brazil, Spain or Germany return to glory? And then there's the underdog question could Ghana, Panama, or even the United States pull off the kind of run that shocks the world? FIFA expects 5 million travelers for the World Cup, with those visitors projected to generate as much as $40 billion in economic impact across North America. They're also real stakes. TSA has warned that many of the agency's new hires may not be ready to staff checkpoints in time for an event of this size. And with ice also expected to have a heavy presence. There are growing questions about whether Americans and even legal visitors could face detention, heightened scrutiny or be removed from matches altogether. In Mexico, authorities plan to deploy 100,000 security personnel due to concerns pertaining to cartel violence. So while the World Cup promises tens of billions of dollars, it also comes with pressure. Can North America deliver a tournament without security failures or political drama overshadowing the games? If it all comes together at the 2026 World Cup won't just be the biggest in history. It could also be the most valuable. Now we're just waiting for the action to begin.
